questions.text: A company that owns or controls production in more than one nation is called a:
questions.explanation: MNCs set up offices and factories for production in regions where they can get cheap labour and other resources.

questions.text: MNCs set up production units close to markets to:
options: Reduce cost of transport and be close to consumers
questions.explanation: Proximity to markets and availability of cheap skilled/unskilled labour are primary factors for MNC location.

questions.text: The money that is spent to buy assets such as land, building, and machines by MNCs is called:
questions.explanation: Investment made by MNCs is called foreign investment, made with the hope of earning profits.

questions.text: Which of the following is a way in which MNCs spread their production?
options: Setting up partnerships with local companies
questions.explanation: MNCs use various strategies to control production across different countries.

questions.text: The most common route for MNC investments is to:
options: Buy up local companies and then expand production
questions.explanation: Buying established local companies allows MNCs to quickly gain a large market share and infrastructure.

questions.text: Cargill Foods, a very large MNC from ______, bought over Parakh Foods in India.
questions.explanation: Cargill Foods expanded its edible oil production in India by acquiring Parakh Foods.

questions.text: Foreign trade creates an opportunity for producers to reach beyond:
questions.explanation: Foreign trade allows producers to sell their goods in markets located in other countries.

questions.text: Globalisation is the process of rapid ______ between countries.
questions.explanation: Globalisation involves more goods, services, investments, and technology moving between countries.

questions.text: The integration of markets and production across countries is being facilitated by:
questions.explanation: MNCs are the major agents facilitating the process of globalisation through investments and trade.

questions.text: What has been a major factor that has enabled globalisation?
options: Improvement in technology, especially in transport and IT
questions.explanation: Faster transport and the telecommunications/internet revolution have made global connectivity easy and cheap.

questions.text: Removing barriers or restrictions set by the government is what is known as:
questions.explanation: Liberalisation allows businesses to make decisions freely about what they wish to import or export.

questions.text: Tax on imports is an example of a:
questions.explanation: Governments use trade barriers to increase or decrease foreign trade and to protect domestic industries.

questions.text: India decided to remove barriers on foreign trade and investment around the year:
questions.explanation: In 1991, India initiated major economic reforms to integrate with the global economy.

questions.text: WTO stands for:
questions.explanation: The World Trade Organisation aims to liberalise international trade.

questions.text: Where is the headquarters of the World Trade Organisation (WTO) located?
questions.explanation: The WTO is headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland.

questions.text: The main aim of the WTO is to:
options: Establish rules for international trade and ensure they are obeyed
questions.explanation: WTO works towards removing barriers to trade and facilitating global trade according to agreed rules.

questions.text: Who benefits the most from globalisation among consumers?
questions.explanation: Wealthy urban consumers have benefitted from a greater choice of high-quality products at lower prices.

questions.text: Several of the top Indian companies like Tata Motors, Infosys, and Ranbaxy have emerged as:
questions.explanation: Globalisation has enabled some large Indian companies to expand their operations worldwide.

questions.text: What are SEZs (Special Economic Zones)?
options: Zones where no taxes are paid by people
Industrial zones with world-class facilities to attract foreign investment
questions.explanation: SEZs provide electricity, water, roads, and transport, along with tax exemptions for companies setting up units there.

questions.text: Companies setting up units in SEZs do not have to pay taxes for an initial period of:
questions.explanation: As per government policy, tax holidays for 5 years are provided to attract investment in SEZs.

questions.text: One major impact of globalisation on workers has been:
options: Flexibility in labour laws leading to job insecurity
questions.explanation: To attract investment, governments have allowed companies to ignore labour laws, making jobs temporary and insecure.

questions.text: The call for 'Fair Globalisation' aims at:
options: Creating opportunities for all and ensuring benefits are shared better
questions.explanation: Fair globalisation ensures that the interests of the developing world and workers are protected.

questions.text: What has been the role of the Indian government in the struggle for fair globalisation?
options: Negotiating at the WTO for fairer rules
questions.explanation: The government can use trade barriers, negotiate for fairer rules, and align with other developing nations.

questions.text: Globalisation has led to intense competition, which has hit which group the hardest?
questions.explanation: Small scale industries (like batteries, capacitors, plastics) have struggled to compete with cheaper imports.

questions.text: Foreign trade leads to ______ of prices of similar goods across two markets.
questions.explanation: Trade allows goods to travel, and competition tends to bring the prices of similar goods closer together in different countries.

questions.text: In the context of globalisation, 'outsourcing' refers to:
options: Contracting out business processes to third-party providers (often in other countries)
Moving headquarters to a new city
questions.explanation: India is a major hub for outsourcing (BPOs) due to cheap and skilled labour.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a benefit of MNCs to host countries?
questions.explanation: While MNCs bring capital and tech, they don't always use local raw materials; they often import from their global network.

questions.text: A 'Quota' is a type of:
questions.explanation: Quotas limit the amount of a specific good that can be imported into a country.

questions.text: Information and Communication Technology (IT) has played a major role in:
questions.explanation: IT allows services like data entry, accounting, and administrative tasks to be done globally.

questions.text: The 'Choice of Consumers' in the market has ______ due to globalisation.
questions.explanation: Consumers now have access to a wide variety of global brands and latest products.

questions.text: WTO is criticized by developing countries because:
options: Developed countries have unfairly retained trade barriers and subsidies
questions.explanation: While WTO forces developing countries to remove barriers, developed countries often keep their own protections (like agri-subsidies).

questions.text: Liberalisation of trade allows ______ to flow more easily between nations.
questions.explanation: Liberalisation reduces friction in the movement of resources globally.

questions.text: An example of a service that has benefitted from globalisation is:
questions.explanation: The service sector, especially those involving IT, has seen the most growth.

questions.text: Globalisation has led to the creation of ______ for companies providing services.
questions.explanation: Companies in IT, call centres, and software development have found massive global markets.

questions.text: Why do MNCs look for countries with flexible labour laws?
options: To reduce labour costs and hire/fire easily
questions.explanation: Flexible laws allow MNCs to reduce their operational costs and stay competitive.

questions.text: Ford Motors entered the Indian market in 1995 in collaboration with:
questions.explanation: Ford set up a plant near Chennai in partnership with Mahindra and Mahindra.

questions.text: Which of the following describes the impact of globalisation on the garment industry?
options: Intense competition among exporters leading to cost-cutting on labour
questions.explanation: Exporters try to cut costs by paying workers less and increasing work hours to meet MNC demands.

questions.text: The process of globalisation has been aided by the ______ of the world economy.
questions.explanation: Liberalisation opened up markets that were previously closed or restricted.

questions.text: MNCs are able to control production in remote locations by:
options: Placing orders with small producers and selling under their own brand names
questions.explanation: Garments, footwear, and sports items are often produced by small producers for large MNCs.

questions.text: What is the primary motive of a Multi-National Corporation?
options: To earn maximum profit by minimising costs
questions.explanation: Economic profit is the driving force behind MNC expansion and operations.
